story:
Weeks after twenty-six-year-old Ben Hoffmann was found dead in a burned-out cabin in the woods, the town still talks about him as if no one can quite explain what happened. I stay silent because I know exactly how Ben died. I killed him.

That night, I was alone in a back booth, ready to call it a night, when a shy-looking guy approached me with tears in his eyes. He said his girlfriend had been in the bathroom too long and begged me to check on her. He called himself Jason. He looked harmless, and concern for the girl tugged at me, so I went. The bathroom was empty. As I turned to tell him, something cracked against my skull, and darkness swallowed me.

I woke tied and gagged in a windowless room—wooden walls, a stinking bucket, a thin mattress. He came in with a handsaw and a smile that didn’t reach his eyes. “You’re a special one,” he said. “We have time. We shouldn’t rush.” Then he locked the door and left. Panic simmered, but I started searching, ripping at the mattress until I worked loose a small metal spring and hid it in my pocket.

He returned wearing that fake gentleman mask. He offered me a choice—easy or hard—and reached to stroke my hair. I kicked him. A syringe clattered from his hand. He slammed me to the floor and squeezed my throat until the room tunneled. With the last of my strength, I jammed the spring into his leg, then drove it into his eye. He howled, flailed, tried to strike me. I hooked the handsaw with my bound wrists and dragged it against the rope until my hands came free. When I was free, he tried to bargain, to call it a mistake. I echoed his own line back to him: we had time; we shouldn’t rush. Then I took up the saw and made sure he would never hurt anyone again.

Outside the room, the place revealed itself as a lonely cabin in the woods. In a padlocked shed, his key opened a small museum of horrors—walls of photos of mutilated women, hair in jars, a bowl I didn’t look into twice. Eleven victims stared back at me. On the news—his face in a photo, his girlfriend pleading—“Jason” became Ben Hoffmann, the town’s missing nice guy. If she’d been his bait, I felt a flicker of pity for her too.

I couldn’t risk my fingerprints or my past. I doused the cabin in gasoline, lit a match, and watched the flames take what I’d touched. The shed, I left intact. From a burner phone, I called 911, gave them the location, warned them about the fire, and described the evidence in the shed so no one would mistake it for a prank. Then I hung up.

Maybe I should feel bad. Even monsters have people who mourn them. But his victims deserve justice, and their families deserve answers. Ben’s mistake was keeping his sins in one place; I’ve learned to keep mine separate. I drove away at dawn, Spandau Ballet on the radio, the spring I used to survive warm in my palm. It will fit with the others. Survival and pleasure, I’ve learned, can look a lot alike.
